body_text: Principles form the basis of methods, techniques, methodologies and tools
clean_text: Principles form the basis of methods, techniques, methodologies and tools

bullets: Seven important principles that may be used in all phases of software development • Rigor and formality
Modularity is the cornerstone principle supporting software design
clean_text: INTRODUCING THE ISSUES Seven important principles that may be used in all phases of software development • Rigor and formality Separation of concerns Modularity Abstraction Anticipation of change Generality Incremental Development Modularity is the cornerstone principle supporting software design
Rigor and formality Separation of concerns Modularity Abstraction Anticipation of change Generality Incremental Development

bullets: To dominate the complexity, separate the issues to concentrate on one at a time
Supports parallelization/synchronisations of efforts and separation of responsibilities
clean_text: Separation of concerns Rigor and formality Separation of concerns Modularity Abstraction Anticipation of change Generality Incremental Development To dominate the complexity, separate the issues to concentrate on one at a time "Divide & conquer" (divide et impera) Supports parallelization/synchronisations of efforts and separation of responsibilities
Keep product requirements separate functionality performance user interface and usability
Time Life cycle models Qualities (qualitative analysis of software) Views activity versus control Problem Domain from Implementation Domain

bullets: A complex system (S) may be divided into simpler subsystems called modules (M).
A system (S) that is composed of modules (M1 … Mn) is called modular.
dealing with a module we can ignore details of other modules
clean_text: Modularity Rigor and formality Separation of concerns Modularity Abstraction Anticipation of change Generality Incremental Development A complex system (S) may be divided into simpler subsystems called modules (M). A system (S) that is composed of modules (M1 … Mn) is called modular. Modularity supports separation of concerns dealing with a module we can ignore details of other modules

bullets: Each module Mi from M1 …. Mn should be highly cohesive
module Mi is a "self-contained" meaningful unit, a system itself, which is a subsystem of a given system S.
Components of a module are closely related to one another
modules have low interactions with others
code_lines: # Cohesion and coupling: how to tackle these
clean_text: Each module Mi from M1 …. Mn should be highly cohesive module Mi is a "self-contained" meaningful unit, a system itself, which is a subsystem of a given system S. Components of a module are closely related to one another Modules should exhibit low coupling modules have low interactions with others understandable separately

bullets: modules should have low interactions with others (otherwise, they cannot be designed on their own, e.g. abstraction from the other modules and the connectivity would not work)
code_lines: # Cohesion and coupling: how to tackle these
clean_text: On the contrary, Modules should exhibit low coupling modules should have low interactions with others (otherwise, they cannot be designed on their own, e.g. abstraction from the other modules and the connectivity would not work) Modules are understandable separately

body_text: (a) Avoid: high coupling of the modules M1 – M3 and low cohesion within them
(b) Target: high cohesion within modules M1 – M3 and low coupling
clean_text: (a) Avoid: high coupling of the modules M1 – M3 and low cohesion within them (b) Target: high cohesion within modules M1 – M3 and low coupling

bullets: Identify the important aspects of a phenomenon and ignore its details
Linked closely to separation of concerns
Type of abstraction to apply depends on purpose
Example: the user interface of a watch (its buttons) abstracts from the watch's internals for the purpose of setting time.
clean_text: Abstraction Rigor and formality Separation of concerns Modularity Abstraction Anticipation of change Generality Incremental Development Identify the important aspects of a phenomenon and ignore its details Linked closely to separation of concerns Type of abstraction to apply depends on purpose Example: the user interface of a watch (its buttons) abstracts from the watch's internals for the purpose of setting time.

bullets: Abstraction: we have done it already many times!
You make your own abstraction introducing the context of the system,
You make your abstractions when prioritise requirements
You make your abstractions when you define data types, i.e. defining the precision
clean_text: Abstraction: we have done it already many times! You make your own abstraction introducing the context of the system, You make your abstractions when prioritise requirements You make your abstractions when you define data types, i.e. defining the precision

bullets: Acceptance testing, alpha and beta testing
clean_text: functions, member functions etc classes, les etc libraries etc Unit testing: Module testing: Sub-system testing: System testing Integration testing Acceptance testing, alpha and beta testing Review and Maintenance

bullets: <sup>l</sup> Rather than deliver the system in a single attempt, the development and delivery is broken down into increments with each increment delivering part of the required functionality
<sup>l</sup> User requirements are prioritised and the highest priority requirements are included in early increments
<sup>l</sup> Once the development of an increment is started, the requirements are frozen though requirements for later increments can continue to evolve
clean_text: Incremental development <sup>l</sup> Rather than deliver the system in a single attempt, the development and delivery is broken down into increments with each increment delivering part of the required functionality <sup>l</sup> User requirements are prioritised and the highest priority requirements are included in early increments <sup>l</sup> Once the development of an increment is started, the requirements are frozen though requirements for later increments can continue to evolve

bullets: <sup>l</sup> Customer value can be delivered with each increment so system functionality is available earlier
<sup>l</sup> Early increments act as a prototype to help elicit requirements for later increments
<sup>l</sup> Lower risk of overall project failure
<sup>l</sup> The highest priority system services tend to receive the most testing
clean_text: Incremental development advantages <sup>l</sup> Customer value can be delivered with each increment so system functionality is available earlier <sup>l</sup> Early increments act as a prototype to help elicit requirements for later increments <sup>l</sup> Lower risk of overall project failure <sup>l</sup> The highest priority system services tend to receive the most testing
